data.title: Vistra prices $2.25B private bond offering
data.description:
• Vistra (VST) priced a $2.25B private bond offering, issuing $1B of senior secured notes due 2031 and $1.25B due 2036, priced at 99.954% and 99.745% of face value, respectively.
  • The 2031 notes will bear interest at the rate of 4.700% per annum, and the 2036 notes will bear interest at 5.350% per annum.
  • The company intends to use the proceeds from the offering to fund a portion of the consideration for the acquisition of Cogentrix Energy; for general corporate purposes, including to repay existing indebtedness; and/or to pay fees and expenses related to the offering.
  • The sale is expected to close on January 22, 2026, subject to customary closing conditions.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: AbbVie secures tariff relief in drug pricing deal, pledges $100B investment
data.description:
AbbVie (ABBV) has reached a three-year deal with the Trump administration to reduce drug prices and invest $100 billion in U.S. research and development (R&D) and capital investments, including manufacturing, over the next decade in exchange for an exemption from certain tariffs.

The drugmaker will provide low prices in Medicaid and also expand direct-to-patient offerings through a government-run website, TrumpRx, for widely used medicines, including Alphagan, Combigan, Humira, and Synthroid.

The deal, which addresses all four of the President's drug pricing priorities, was enabled by the Trump administration providing exemption from tariffs and future price mandates, AbbVie (ABBV) said, adding that further terms remain confidential.

The Trump administration has pushed drugmakers to cut prices, noting that U.S. consumers often pay nearly three times more for prescription drugs than patients in other developed countries.

In December, the administration reached pricing agreements with several major pharmaceutical companies—including Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY), Gilead Sciences (GILD), Amgen (AMGN), and Merck (MRK) —to lower cut prices of certain prescription drugs in the U.S.

Back in November, Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Eli Lilly (LLY) announced a major drug-pricing deal with the Trump administration, receiving relief from tariffs in exchange for lower prices for their branded medicines, including their popular weight loss drugs, in the U.S.

Among major drugmakers, Pfizer (PFE) and AstraZeneca (AZN) have already inked agreements with the White House to provide some of their prescription drugs at significant discounts to cash-paying customers through TrumpRx.

Source: Press Release

data.title: Energy Transfer prices $3B bond offering
data.description:
Energy Transfer (ET) priced a $3 billion bond offering, issuing $1 billion each of senior notes due in 2031, 2036, and 2056, with interest rates of 4.55%, 5.35%, and 6.30%, and offering prices of 99.830%, 99.933%, and 99.842% of face value, respectively.

Energy Transfer priced a $3 billion bond offering, issuing $1 billion each of senior notes due in 2031, 2036, and 2056, with interest rates of 4.55%, 5.35%, and 6.30%, respectively.

The offering is expected to settle on January 27, 2026, subject to the satisfaction of customary closing conditions.

Energy Transfer intends to use the net proceeds of around $2.97 billion (before offering expenses) to refinance existing indebtedness, including to repay commercial paper and borrowings under its revolving credit facility, and for general partnership purposes.

BofA Securities, Deutsche Bank Securities, Mizuho, MUFG and SMBC Nikko are acting as joint book-running managers for the senior notes offering.

Source: Press Release

data.title: Aldi plots next phase of aggressive U.S. expansion
data.description:
Aldi is celebrating its 50th year in the United States this year with plans to keep expanding. The discount grocer is pursuing an aggressive expansion strategy with plans to open more than 180 new stores across 31 states by the end of 2026. That pace of growth would bring Aldi's total U.S. store count to nearly 2,800 locations, positioning the company to reach its ambitious target of 3,200 stores by the end of 2028.

The expansion of the Aldi chain has been fueled by strong customer demand, with 17 million new customers visiting Aldi stores in 2025. Looking ahead, the company will enter Maine as its 40th state and launch a five-year plan to enter Colorado, with more than 50 stores planned for Denver and Colorado Springs. In Phoenix, Aldi will open 10 new stores in 2026 with plans to reach 40 locations by 2030, while the Las Vegas region will see the company double its current store count by 2030.

To support the higher store count, Aldi is expanding its distribution network by 20%, with new centers planned in Florida, Arizona, and Colorado over the next three years.

Aldi has a dual corporate structure, split in 1961 into the separate family-owned Aldi South and Aldi Nord businesses, which operate in different geographic regions. The company has its U.S. headquarters located in Batavia, Illinois. Aldi management has consistently signaled that it can fund its expansion plans without public equity, so analysts do not expect an IPO announcement.

data.title: Diamondback Energy signals lower prices for oil production in Q4
data.description:
Diamondback Energy (FANG) said post-market Monday it received lower prices for its oil production in Q4 compared to the preceding three months, according to an 8-K filing.

The company said its average realized prices for its total oil production dropped to $58.00/bbl in Q4 from $64.60/bbl in Q3, and its average realized prices for natural gas after hedging fell to $1.03/Mcf compared to $1.75/Mcf in Q3.

Crude oil prices declined more than 9% in Q4, as concerns about oversupply and tariffs outweighed geopolitical risks.

Diamondback's (FANG) realized oil and natural gas liquids prices were modestly weaker than expected, while natural gas realizations were largely in line with expectations, Siebert Williams Shank analyst Gabriele Sorbara said following the results.

"Obviously, this will put a little pressure on the bottom line for 4Q25, but Street expectations have to adjust for the sector, as analysts mark-to-market their models for actuals," Sorbara wrote.

Diamondback (FANG), which will release its financial results on February 23, is expected to report adjusted earnings of $2.64/share for Q4 and $12.98/share for the full year, according to data compiled by LSEG.

data.title: Italy seeks €7B in damages from ArcelorMittal over ILVA steelworks - FT
data.description:
Italian administrators overseeing Europe's largest steelworks have sued its former owner
ArcelorMittal (MT), seeking €7B (~$8.1B) in damages, the Financial Times reported Monday, citing a complaint filed with a Milan court this month.

In the complaint, which would be one of the largest compensation claims ever heard in an Italian court, Acciaierie d’Italia is seeking compensation for what it alleges was ArcelorMittal’s (MT) "mismanagement" of the steelworks, formerly known as Ilva.

The steelworks faced a criminal prosecution in 2012 over its environmental record, and the Italian government seized control of the site from ArcelorMittal (MT) nearly two years ago, placing it under special administration by state-appointed commissioners; the government and the company have been locked in a long-running dispute over the original commitments to finance the plant's environmental clean-up.

Successive governments have poured billions of euros in public funds into Acciaierie d’Italia to keep it afloat and save the jobs of its 10,000 staff, seeing the plant as a strategic asset critical for Italy's long-term industrial security.

Last month, U.S.-based investment funds Bedrock Industries and Flacks Group submitted
binding offers for the ailing company.

data.title: Lynas Rare Earths CEO Lacaze to step down after 12 years in role
data.description:
Lynas Rare Earths (LYSCF) (LYSDY) said Monday that CEO Amanda Lacaze will retire at the end of the financial year through June, stepping down after 12 years leading the world's largest supplier of critical minerals outside of China.

Lacaze, one of just a handful of female CEOs in the mining industry, turned around a company that was on the brink of collapse when she took over in 2014 into a dominant force in the production of rare earth elements, with its stock surging more than 12x during her tenure and its market value soaring to nearly A$15B from ~A$400M when she began.

Lacaze had little experience in the resources sector when she became CEO, joining Lynas (LYSCF) (LYSDY) from the telecommunications industry, having served as the managing director of marketing at Telstra, Australia's largest telecom firm, while also previously holding positions at Nestle.

Lynas (LYSCF) (LYSDY) said it will consider both internal and external candidates to succeed Lacaze.

data.title: PG&E reaches $100M shareholder settlement over 2017-18 California wildfires
data.description:
PG&E (PCG) reached a $100M settlement with shareholders who accused the utility company of misleading them about its wildfire prevention and safety protocols ahead of California wildfires in 2017 and 2018, according to a preliminary settlement filed Saturday with the U.S. District Court in San Jose that requires a judge's approval.

Shareholders led by the Public Employees Retirement Association of New Mexico said PG&E (PCG) concealed its defective wildfire safety practices, including electrical equipment and vegetation management blamed for starting or exacerbating the 2017 North Bay fires and 2018 Camp Fire.

The North Bay fires included the Tubbs Fire, which killed 22 people and destroyed more than 5,600 structures, and the Camp Fire killed 85 people and destroyed more than 18,800 structures, including most of the town of Paradise.

The company denied wrongdoing in agreeing to settle, but called the settlement "a significant step forward in resolving claims from the 2017 and 2018 wildfires, as we continue our work to reduce wildfire risk across our energy system."

data.title: Iran's top trading partners that could face new tariffs
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump said he is imposing a 25% tariff on goods from any country "doing business" with Iran.

“Effective immediately, any Country doing business with the Islamic Republic of Iran will pay a Tariff of 25% on any and all business being done with the United States of America. This Order is final and conclusive,” Trump posted.

Here are Iran's biggest trading partners:

data.title: Goldman forecasts lower crude prices in 2026 on continued oversupply
data.description:
Oil prices likely will drift lower this year as a wave of supply creates a market surplus, although geopolitical risks tied to Russia, Venezuela and Iran will continue to drive volatile price swings, Goldman Sachs analysts said in a note Sunday.

"Rising global oil stocks and our forecast of a 2.3M bbl/day surplus in 2026 suggest that rebalancing the market likely requires lower oil prices in 2026 to slow down non-OPEC supply growth and support solid demand growth, barring large supply disruptions or OPEC production cuts," Goldman wrote, forecasting Brent and WTI crude will average $56/bbl and $52/bbl, respectively, this year, bottoming at a respective $54/bbl and $50/bbl in Q4, before rising modestly in 2027 to a respective average of $58/bbl and $54/bbl amid solid demand and slowing supply growth from countries outside of OPEC.

"We assume that U.S. policymakers' preference for strong energy supply keeps sustained oil price upside in check ahead of the mid-term [elections]," Goldman analysts noted.

The bank said it is still "long-run constructive" on crude oil because of expectations of demand through 2040, in part because of stronger petrochemical, aviation and road transportation demand, given limited alternatives.

Goldman released a survey last week that said 59% of more than 1,100 clients across asset classes are bearish or slightly bearish on crude, placing sentiment just off record lows in a monthly dataset dating back to January 2016, as the global market anticipates a glut.

Adding to broader bearish sentiment, a record number of institutional investors said oil was their favorite short, according to the survey, which was conducted January 5-7 by Goldman's Marquee MarketView.

Crude futures edged higher for a third straight session Monday, with tensions in Iran keeping a bid in the market, as President Trump weighed responses to a deadly crackdown on one the stiffest challenges to clerical rule since ‌the 1979 Islamic Revolution.

"The prospect of disruption to Iranian energy output presents a bigger threat to global supplies than the situation in Venezuela, particularly given the potential for instability to affect supply outside Iran itself," Capital Economics said in a note. "The geopolitical stakes are high too given Iran's current status as one of the largest economies in China's current sphere of influence," as Iranian crude accounts for nearly 15% of China's total crude imports, compared to Venezuela's 2%.

The analysts said a strike by oil workers, a blockade of the shadow fleet, or threats to close the Strait of Hormuz could push oil prices up $15-$20/bbl, although the increase would likely be short-lived.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for February delivery ended +0.6% to $59.50/bbl, its highest settlement value since December 5, and front-month Brent March crude (CO1:COM) finished +0.8% to $63.87/bbl, its best close since November 18.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) jumped after weekend forecasts added colder weather to the outlook, with a corresponding rise expected in heating demand; the front-month February Nymex contract closed +7.6% to $3.409/MMBtu.
